title: How does CPython execute your code?
description: From source to bytecode to the eval loop.
answer: CPython tokenizes source into an AST, compiles it to bytecode, and the interpreter's eval loop executes that bytecode on a stack-based virtual machine.

title: What is the LEGB rule for scope resolution?
answer: Python resolves a name by searching Local, then Enclosing, then Global, then Built-in scopes, using the first match.

title: How does Python's name and object reference model work?
answer: Variables are names bound to objects on the heap; assignment binds a name to an object and never copies, so multiple names can alias the same object.

title: How does attribute lookup work on a Python object?
answer: Reading obj.attr checks data descriptors on the type, then the instance dict, then the class and its MRO, then falls back to __getattr__.

title: How does the Python import system work?
answer: import checks the sys.modules cache first, then finders locate the module and a loader executes it once, caching the resulting module object.

title: Why does Python cache small integers?
answer: CPython pre-creates and reuses int objects from -5 to 256, so identical small ints share one object, which is why 'is' can surprise you on numbers.

title: What is string interning in Python?
answer: CPython stores certain strings (identifiers, short literals) in an intern pool so equal strings share one object, speeding up dict/identifier comparisons.

title: How does a Python list grow in memory?
answer: A list stores a resizable array of pointers and over-allocates capacity geometrically, so append is amortized O(1) rather than reallocating every time.

title: How does a Python set find items?
answer: A set is an open-addressing hash table: it hashes the element to a slot and probes forward on collision, giving average O(1) membership.

title: What is the difference between the call stack and the heap in Python?
answer: Frames (locals, the call chain) live on the call stack; all objects live on the heap, and names in frames just reference those heap objects.

title: What is the descriptor protocol?
answer: A descriptor is an object defining __get__/__set__/__delete__; it customizes attribute access and is how methods, property, classmethod, and staticmethod work.

title: How does a for loop work under the hood?
answer: A for loop calls iter() on the iterable once, then next() repeatedly until StopIteration is raised, binding each yielded value to the loop variable.

title: What is a generator's lifecycle?
answer: A generator moves through created -> suspended (at each yield) -> running -> closed/exhausted, retaining local state between resumes until StopIteration or close().

title: How does yield from delegation work?
answer: yield from delegates iteration (and sent values/exceptions) to a sub-iterator, transparently piping its values out and its return value back to the caller.

title: How does a + b dispatch in Python?
answer: a + b first tries a.__add__(b); if that returns NotImplemented (or b is a subclass), Python tries the reflected b.__radd__(a).

title: What is the __hash__ and __eq__ contract?
answer: Objects that compare equal must have equal hashes, and overriding __eq__ without __hash__ makes a class unhashable; immutability keeps the hash stable.

title: How does the in operator work in Python?
answer: x in obj calls obj.__contains__(x) if defined, else iterates the object comparing each item, giving O(1) for sets/dicts but O(n) for lists.

title: What is the difference between __getattr__ and __getattribute__?
answer: __getattribute__ intercepts every attribute access; __getattr__ is the fallback called only when normal lookup fails (raises AttributeError).

title: What are bound and unbound methods in Python?
answer: Accessing a function through an instance returns a bound method (self pre-filled) because functions are descriptors; accessing it on the class gives the plain function.

title: How do closures capture variables in Python?
answer: A closure captures enclosing variables by reference through cell objects shared between the outer and inner function, not by copying their values.

title: What is the difference between class variables and instance variables?
answer: Class variables are shared by all instances and stored on the class; instance variables live in each instance's __dict__ and shadow the class variable on assignment.

title: What are mixins in Python?
answer: A mixin is a small class that adds focused behavior to others through multiple inheritance, without being instantiated on its own.

title: What is duck typing in Python?
answer: Duck typing means an object's suitability is judged by the methods/attributes it has, not its class: 'if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck.'

title: What is monkey patching in Python?
answer: Monkey patching replaces or adds attributes/methods on a class or module at runtime, useful for testing or hotfixes but risky for maintainability.

title: What is composition versus inheritance?
answer: Inheritance models 'is-a' and shares implementation down a hierarchy; composition models 'has-a' by holding other objects, giving looser coupling and more flexibility.
